title: Recipe Scaling Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate scaled ingredient amounts instantly. Enter original servings, target servings, and up to 10 ingredients. Smart unit conversion included.
intro: Need to double a dinner recipe or cut a batch of cookies in half? This calculator scales every ingredient proportionally in one step. Enter your original serving count, your target, and up to 10 ingredients with amounts and units. **Smart unit conversion** automatically switches cups to tablespoons when results fall below ¼ cup, and ounces to pounds when they exceed 16 oz. Note: baking staples like eggs and leavening agents don't scale perfectly — see the limitations section below.
keyTakeaway: Scaling from 4 to 12 servings triples every ingredient: 1 cup flour becomes 3 cups, 2 tsp baking powder becomes 6 tsp — but eggs may need rounding and leavening should be increased by only 75–80% of the linear amount.
useCases: Doubling a family pasta recipe for a dinner party
Halving a cake recipe to fit a 6-inch pan instead of a 9-inch
Scaling a restaurant prep recipe from 50 to 200 servings
Adjusting a cocktail batch from 1 drink to a pitcher of 8
Converting a personal meal-prep recipe to a full week of lunches
Reducing a cookie recipe to a small test batch before baking in bulk
explanation:
## How It Works

Recipe scaling uses a single **scale factor** applied to every ingredient:

```
Scale Factor = Desired Servings ÷ Original Servings
Scaled Amount = Original Amount × Scale Factor
```

For example, scaling from 4 to 10 servings gives a factor of 2.5. An ingredient listed as 1 cup becomes 2.5 cups.

## Smart Unit Conversion Rules

Raw scaled values are automatically converted to more practical units:

| Condition | Conversion Applied |
|---|---|
| Scaled cups < 0.25 | Convert to tablespoons (1 cup = 16 tbsp) |
| Scaled tablespoons < 1 | Convert to teaspoons (1 tbsp = 3 tsp) |
| Scaled oz ≥ 16 | Convert to pounds (16 oz = 1 lb) |
| Scaled kg ≥ 1 | Display as kg with 2 decimals |
| Scaled L ≥ 1 | Display as L with 2 decimals |

## Worked Example

Original recipe: **4 servings**. Target: **10 servings**.

- Scale factor: 10 ÷ 4 = **2.5×**
- 2 cups flour → 5 cups flour
- 1 tsp baking powder → 2.5 tsp (linear) → **adjusted to ~2 tsp** (leavening cap at 75–80%)
- 2 eggs → 5 eggs (rounded to nearest whole)
- 4 oz butter → 10 oz butter (displayed as **0.625 lb**)
- 3 tbsp sugar → 7.5 tbsp → displayed as **7 tbsp + 1½ tsp** (approximate)

## Limitations & When NOT to Apply Linear Scaling

**Leavening agents (baking powder, baking soda, yeast):** Scaling linearly beyond 2× frequently produces over-risen, bitter, or dense results. Professional bakers cap leavening increases at 75–80% of the linear amount when scaling up 3× or more.

**Eggs:** Since eggs come in discrete units, fractional results must be rounded. A half-egg can be approximated by beating one egg and using half the volume (~1.5 tbsp per half large egg).

**Salt and strong spices:** Taste perception is not strictly linear. Scale to 75% of the calculated amount first, then adjust by taste.

**Pan size:** Doubling a batter recipe does not mean doubling bake time. Volume-to-surface-area ratios change; check doneness by internal temperature or toothpick test.

**Cook times:** This calculator does NOT adjust cooking times. Larger batches in the same vessel may require 10–25% more time; smaller batches may need less.

faq.q: What is a scale factor and how is it calculated?
faq.a: The scale factor is the ratio of desired servings to original servings. If you want 12 servings from a 4-serving recipe, the scale factor is 12 ÷ 4 = 3. Every ingredient is multiplied by this number.

faq.q: Why shouldn't I scale baking powder and baking soda linearly?
faq.a: Leavening works through a chemical reaction with a ceiling effect. Too much baking powder produces a bitter taste and can cause a cake to collapse. For recipes scaling 3× or more, use 75–80% of the linearly calculated amount and test before committing to a large batch.

faq.q: How do I handle a scaled egg amount that isn't a whole number?
faq.a: Round to the nearest whole egg for most recipes. If precision matters (custards, soufflés), beat a whole egg, weigh or measure it (a large egg ≈ 3 tbsp / 50 g), then use the exact fractional volume.

faq.q: Does scaling change cooking time or oven temperature?
faq.a: No. Temperature stays the same. Time may increase by 10–25% for larger batches in the same vessel, or decrease for smaller batches. Always verify doneness with a thermometer or visual cues, not a timer alone.

faq.q: Can I scale a recipe down to less than one serving?
faq.a: Yes. A scale factor below 1 (e.g., 0.25 for a quarter batch) works mathematically. Practically, very small amounts of leavening are hard to measure accurately; use a precision kitchen scale for anything under ¼ tsp.

faq.q: Why does the calculator convert cups to tablespoons automatically?
faq.a: Measuring ⅛ cup is awkward and imprecise; 2 tablespoons is the same volume and far easier to measure accurately. The conversion threshold is 0.25 cups (4 tablespoons).

faq.q: Is this calculator suitable for professional or commercial recipe scaling?
faq.a: It handles the arithmetic correctly. For commercial kitchens, also account for equipment capacity, batch cooking losses (evaporation, trim waste), and ingredient purchasing units. The baking non-linearity notes are especially important at high multipliers (5× and above).

faq.q: What units does the calculator support?
faq.a: US volume (cups, tablespoons, teaspoons), US weight (oz, lb), metric weight (g, kg), metric volume (ml, L), and whole-unit counts (eggs, pieces). Mixed US/metric in the same recipe is supported ingredient by ingredient.

faq.q: How accurate is the smart unit display for tablespoons and teaspoons?
faq.a: The conversion is exact (1 cup = 16 tbsp; 1 tbsp = 3 tsp). Rounding to 2 decimal places is applied for display. For baking, always measure by weight when possible — 1 cup of flour can range from 120 g to 150 g depending on scooping technique.

faq.q: Can I use this for drinks and cocktail batches?
faq.a: Yes. Cocktail scaling is purely linear — no leavening concerns. Scale factor works identically. Use ml or oz units for liquid ingredients. Remember ice dilution changes as batch size increases in punches and large-format cocktails.

sources.name: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service — Cooking for Groups
FDA — Food Code 2022: Volume and Weight Equivalents
NIST Handbook 44 — Specifications for Measuring Devices (weights and measures)

dataUpdate.notes: Unit conversion constants (cups/tbsp/tsp, oz/lb) are fixed physical standards and do not change. Baking adjustment recommendations reviewed against current professional baking literature as needed.
dataUpdate.source: USDA Food Safety and Inspection Service — Cooking for Groups
